title: पवमान सूक्तम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री पवमान सूक्तम् ॥

ॐ हिरण्यवर्णाः शुचयः पावकाः
यासु जातः कश्यपो यास्विन्द्रः।
अग्निं या गर्भं दधिरे विरूपा-
स्तान ऽआपः शग्गँ स्योना भवन्तु॥१॥

यासाँ राजा वरुणो याति मध्ये
सत्यानृते अवपश्यञ्जनानाम्।
मधुश्चुतः शुचयो याः पावका-
स्तान ऽआपः शग्गँ स्योना भवन्तु॥२॥

यासां देवा दिवि कृण्वन्ति भक्षं
या अन्तरिक्षे बहुधा भवन्ति।
याः पृथिवीं पयसोन्दन्ति शुक्रा-
स्तान ऽआपः शग्गँ स्योना भवन्तु॥३॥

शिवेन मा चक्षुषा पश्यतापः
शिवया तन्वोप स्पृशत त्वचं मे।
सर्वाँ अग्नीँ रप्सुषदो हुवेम
मयि वर्चो बलमोजो निधत्त॥४॥

पवमानः सुवर्जनः पवित्रेण विचर्षणिः।
यः पोतायजत्क्रतुम्॥५॥

पवमानस्य ते वयं पवित्रेण पुनीमहे।
सखे स्तोत्राणि गृह्णतः॥६॥

यत्ते पवित्रमर्चिष्यध्यत्तं विततं दिवः।
भ्राजन्ति अस्य केतवो विभ्राष्टिमर्चये अमूर्॥७॥

स सुतः पृष्ठ्या विपो वाजी धनानि गच्छति।
सो अश्वो न क्रतुर्हिनो ऽभि वाजं अपस्यते॥८॥

ॐ असुन्वन्तं सयावानम-
पशुं चारयन्तम्।
सवनेदो दुरासदम्॥९॥

ॐ आ-ब्रह्मन् ब्राह्मणो ब्रह्मवर्चसी जायतामा-
राष्ट्रे राजन्यः शूर इषव्योऽति-
व्याधी महारथो जायताम्।
दोग्ध्री धेनुर्वोढा-नड्वानाशुः सप्तिः
पुरन्धिर्योषा जिष्णू रथेष्ठाः।
सभेयो युवास्य यजमानस्य वीरो
जायतां निकामे निकामे नः पर्जन्यो वर्षतु
फलवत्यो न ओषधयः पच्यन्तां
योगक्षेमो नः कल्पताम्॥१०॥

ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥

॥ इति ऋग्वेद पवमान सूक्तं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Pavamana Suktam ||

Om Hiranyavarnah Shuchayah Pavakah
Yasu Jatah Kashyapo Yasv-indrah.
Agnim Ya Garbham Dadhire Virupa-
stan Aapah Shaggam Syona Bhavantu.||1||

Yasam Raja Varuno Yati Madhye
Satyanrite Avapashyan Jananam.
Madhushchutah Shuchayo Yah Pavaka-
stan Aapah Shaggam Syona Bhavantu.||2||

Yasam Deva Divi Krinvanti Bhaksham
Ya Antarikse Bahudha Bhavanti.
Yah Prithivim Payaso-ndanti Shukra-
stan Aapah Shaggam Syona Bhavantu.||3||

Shivena Ma Chakshusha Pashyata-apah
Shivaya Tanvo-pa Sprishata Tvacham Me.
Sarvam Agnim Rapsu-sado Huvema
Mayi Varcho Balam-ojo Nidhatta.||4||

Pavamanah Suvar-janah Pavitrena Vicharshanih.
Yah Pota-yajat-kratum.||5||

Pavamanasya Te Vayam Pavitrena Punimehe.
Sakhe Stotrani Grihnatah.||6||

Yat Te Pavitram-archishy-adhyattam Vitatam Divah.
Bhrajanti Asya Ketavo Vibhrashtim-archaye Amur.||7||

Sa Sutah Prishthya Vipo Vaji Dhanani Gachchati.
So Ashvo Na Kratur-hino Abhi Vajam Apasyate.||8||

Om Asunvantam Sayavanam-apashum Charayantam.
Savanedo Durasadam.||9||

Om Aa-Brahman Brahmano Brahmavarchasi Jayatam-aa-
Rashtre Rajanyah Shura Ishavyo-ati-
vyadhi Maharatho Jayatam.
Dogdhri Dhenurvoodha-nadvaan-aashuh Saptih
Purandhir-yosha Jishnu Ratheshthah.
Sabheyo Yuvasya Yajamanasya Viro
Jayatam Nikame Nikame Nah Parjanyo Varshatu
Phalavatyo Na Oshadhayah Pachyantam
Yogakshemo Nah Kalpatam.||10||

Om Shantih Shantih Shantih.

|| Iti Rigveda Pavamana Suktam Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
जो जल स्वर्ण वर्ण के, पवित्र और शुद्ध करने वाले हैं, जिनमें कश्यप उत्पन्न हुए, जिनमें इन्द्र उत्पन्न हुए, जिन्होंने अग्नि को गर्भ में धारण किया — वे जल हमारे लिए सुखकारी हों।

जिनके मध्य में राजा वरुण विचरण करते हैं, मनुष्यों के सत्य-असत्य का अवलोकन करते हुए, जो मधु-रस से प्रवाहित, शुद्ध और पवित्र हैं — वे जल हमारे लिए सुखकारी हों।

जिन जलों का देवता स्वर्ग में सेवन करते हैं, जो अन्तरिक्ष में अनेक रूपों में विद्यमान हैं, जो पृथ्वी पर उज्ज्वल रस सिंचित करते हैं — वे जल हमारे लिए सुखकारी हों।

हे जलो! मुझे शिव (कल्याणकारी) दृष्टि से देखो, अपने शिव शरीर से मेरी त्वचा का स्पर्श करो। मुझमें तेज, बल और ओज स्थापित करो।

हे ब्राह्मणो! इस राष्ट्र में ब्रह्मतेज वाले ब्राह्मण उत्पन्न हों। शूरवीर, महारथी क्षत्रिय उत्पन्न हों। दूध देने वाली गाय, भार ढोने वाला बैल, शीघ्रगामी अश्व हों। हमारे लिए पर्जन्य (बादल) इच्छानुसार वर्षा करे, फलवती औषधियाँ पकें, और हमारा योगक्षेम (प्राप्ति एवं रक्षण) कल्पित हो।
benefits: Sacred Vedic hymn for purification of body, mind, and environment
Invokes divine waters (Apah) for spiritual and physical cleansing
Traditionally associated with prosperity, rainfall, abundant harvests, and national well-being
Recited during Abhisheka (sacred bathing) ceremonies of deities
Classical tradition invokes this for removal of impurities and negative karma through the power of soma pavamana
description: The Pavamana Suktam, known in Sanskrit as पवमान सूक्तम्, is a Vedic hymn in praise of Lord Vishnu. This revered text originates from Rigveda (9th Mandala) and Taittiriya Aranyaka. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to remove all impurities and negative karma through the power of Soma Pavamana as well as bring prosperity, rainfall, abundant harvests, and national well-being. Additionally, it is known to recited during Abhisheka (sacred bathing) ceremonies of deities. With 10 verses and a reading time of about 7 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Pavamana Suktam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Pavamana Suktam from Rigveda in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning and transliteration. Sacred Vedic hymn for purification, prosperity and well-being.
alsoKnownAs: Pavamana Mantra Asato Ma Sad Gamaya

seoFaqs.question: What is the Pavamana Suktam and what is its source in the Vedas?
seoFaqs.answer: The Pavamana Suktam (पवमान सूक्तम् — pavamana = the self-purifying one, an epithet of Soma) is a Vedic hymn dedicated to Soma Pavamana, the divine purifying force, drawn from the entire ninth Mandala of the Rigveda which is exclusively devoted to Soma Pavamana hymns (the only Mandala in the Rigveda dedicated to a single deity). Selected verses are also preserved in the Taittiriya Aranyaka and recited as a separate Pavamana Sukta in the Krishna Yajurveda tradition. The Sukta is most famous for containing — or being closely associated with — the celebrated Pavamana Mantra Asato Ma Sad Gamaya from the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 1.3.28, one of the most universally known Vedic prayers worldwide. (Sources: Rigveda Mandala 9; Taittiriya Aranyaka; Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 1.3.28.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Asato Ma Sad Gamaya Tamaso Ma Jyotir Gamaya Mrityor Ma Amritam Gamaya?
seoFaqs.answer: This celebrated Pavamana Mantra from the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 1.3.28 prays: Asato ma sad gamaya — Lead me from the unreal (asat — that which is impermanent, illusory) to the real (sat — the eternal truth); Tamaso ma jyotir gamaya — Lead me from darkness (tamas — ignorance) to light (jyotis — wisdom); Mrityor ma amritam gamaya — Lead me from death (mrityu — the cycle of birth and death) to immortality (amrita — the deathless state of moksha); Om Shantih Shantih Shantih — Om peace, peace, peace. This is one of the most universally chanted prayers in the entire Hindu tradition, recited at school assemblies, peace conferences, interfaith gatherings, and the opening of every spiritual gathering across the world. (Sources: Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 1.3.28; Pavamana Sukta tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is the ninth Mandala of the Rigveda devoted entirely to Soma Pavamana?
seoFaqs.answer: The Rigveda's ninth Mandala is unique — all 114 hymns of the Mandala are addressed to a single deity, Soma Pavamana (the self-purifying Soma). No other Mandala in the Rigveda is so dedicated. The reason is the central importance of the Soma sacrifice in Vedic ritual: the Soma plant was pressed, filtered through wool (the literal pavamana — flowing pure), mixed with milk and offered to the gods. The hymns of Mandala 9 were chanted by the Udgatri priest during the Soma-pressing ritual, with each verse synchronised to a stage of the pressing. Symbolically, the pavamana of Soma represents the purification of consciousness — the inner Soma of the meditator flowing through the mind to attain immortality. (Sources: Rigveda Mandala 9; Aurobindo's Secret of the Veda; Hindupedia Soma.)

seoFaqs.question: Is the Pavamana Suktam the same as the Pavamana Mantra Asato Ma Sad Gamaya?
seoFaqs.answer: They are related but technically distinct. The Pavamana Suktam refers to the larger collection of Vedic hymns to Soma Pavamana from Rigveda Mandala 9 and selected verses preserved in the Taittiriya Aranyaka, used during Vedic rituals and recitation. The Pavamana Mantra (or Pavamana Abhyaroha Mantra) refers specifically to the three-line prayer Asato Ma Sad Gamaya from the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 1.3.28, which is technically not from the Rigveda but from the Shukla Yajurveda Upanishad. However, in popular usage and many printed compilations, the two are recited together as the Pavamana Sukta tradition, since both share the theme of pavana (purification) and the prayer to be led from impurity to purity. (Sources: Rigveda Mandala 9; Brihadaranyaka Upanishad; classical compilation tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When is the Pavamana Suktam traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Pavamana Sukta and the associated Pavamana Mantra are recited daily during the morning sandhya as part of the purification (achamana) sequence, before commencing any Vedic yajna or ritual to purify the space and the participants, at the opening of every spiritual gathering, retreat or satsang, before commencing meditation, before any auspicious life-event (marriage, griha-pravesha, naming ceremony, upanayanam), during all Soma-yajnas (Agnishtoma, Atiratra), on every Ekadashi and Pradosha, on Maha Shivaratri (Soma being intimately linked with Shiva who wears the moon), and at funeral rites to lead the soul from death to immortality (the third line of the Pavamana Mantra). (Sources: Smarta sandhya paddhati; Vedic yajna manuals; classical recitation tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the symbolic and spiritual meaning of pavamana?
seoFaqs.answer: The Sanskrit word pavamana means literally that which is being purified or that which is self-purifying. In the Vedic ritual context, it refers to the Soma juice flowing through the wool filter — being purified as it flows. In the inner-Vedic spiritual interpretation (championed by Sayana, Aurobindo and the Upanishadic tradition), pavamana is the inner Soma — the flow of pure consciousness through the mind, being purified of impurities (klesha) as it flows toward the supreme. The Pavamana hymns are therefore not merely about a ritual plant — they are meditations on the self-purification of consciousness, on the flow of awareness from the gross to the subtle, from impurity (asat, tamas, mrityu) to purity (sat, jyotis, amritam). This is why they remain among the most chanted Vedic mantras to this day. (Sources: Rigveda Mandala 9; Aurobindo's Hymns to the Mystic Fire; Upanishadic tradition.)
